The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") estimates that the district's population was 705,749 as of July 2019, up more than 100,000 people since the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"). When measured decade-over-decade, this shows growth since 2000, following a half-century of population decline.[[137]](#cite_note-145) Washington was the [24th-most populous place](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Washington,_D.C.&action=edit).[[138]](#cite_note-146) According to data from 2010, commuters from the suburbs boost the district's daytime population past one million.[[139]](#cite_note-147) If the district were a state, it would rank [49th in population](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population"), ahead of [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") and [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming").[[140]](#cite_note-quickfacts-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Washington%2C_DC_%285559893527%29.png/250px-Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Washington%2C_DC_%285559893527%29.png)](/wiki/File:Race_and_ethnicity_2010-_Washington,_DC_(5559893527).png)

Map of racial distribution in the [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"), according to the [2010 U.S. census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"); each dot represents 25 people: **White**, **Black**, **Asian**, **Hispanic** or **Other** (yellow)

The [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"), which includes the district and surrounding suburbs, is the [sixth-largest](/wiki/Table_of_United_States_Metropolitan_Statistical_Areas "Table of United States Metropolitan Statistical Areas") metropolitan area in the U.S., with an estimated six million residents as of 2016.[[141]](#cite_note-149) When the Washington area is included with [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") and its suburbs, it forms the vast [Washington–Baltimore combined statistical area](/wiki/Washington%E2%80%93Baltimore_combined_statistical_area "Washington–Baltimore combined statistical area"). With a population exceeding 9.8 million residents in 2020, it is the [third-largest](/wiki/List_of_United_States_combined_statistical_areas "List of United States combined statistical areas") combined statistical area in the country.[[142]](#cite_note-150)

According to [Department of Housing and Development](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress") in 2022, there were an estimated 4,410 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people in Washington, D.C.[[143]](#cite_note-151)[[144]](#cite_note-152)
The city passed a law in 2013 that requires shelter to be provided to everyone in need when the temperature drops below freezing.[[145]](#cite_note-153) Since D.C. does not have enough shelter units available, every winter it books hotel rooms in the suburbs with an average cost of around $100 for a night. According to the D.C. Department of Human Services, during the winter of 2012 the city spent $2,544,454 on putting homeless families in hotels,[[146]](#cite_note-154) and budgeted $3.2 million on hotel beds in 2013.[[147]](#cite_note-155)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

According to 2020 Census Bureau data, the population of Washington, D.C., was 41.4% Black or African American, 39.6% White (37.9% non-Hispanic White), 4.9% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_American "Asian American"), 0.5% American Indian or Alaska Native, 0.1% Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and 5.4% Some Other Race. Individuals from two or more races made up 8.1% of the population. Hispanics of any race made up 11.3% of the district's population.[[131]](#cite_note-Census2020-136)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Leffler_-_1968_Washington%2C_D.C._Martin_Luther_King%2C_Jr._riots.jpg/250px-Leffler_-_1968_Washington%2C_D.C._Martin_Luther_King%2C_Jr._riots.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leffler_-_1968_Washington,_D.C._Martin_Luther_King,_Jr._riots.jpg)

The city's African American population has declined since the [1968 riots](/wiki/1968_Washington,_D.C.,_riots "1968 Washington, D.C., riots").

Washington, D.C. has had a relatively large [African American population](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_with_large_African-American_populations "List of U.S. cities with large African-American populations") since the city's foundation.[[148]](#cite_note-156) African American residents composed about 30% of the district's total population between 1800 and 1940.[[39]](#cite_note-histpop-42) The black population reached a peak of 70% by 1970 and has since declined as African Americans moved to the surrounding suburbs. Partly as a result of [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification"), there was a 31.4% increase in the non-Hispanic white population and an 11.5% decrease in the black population between 2000 and 2010.[[149]](#cite_note-demshift-157) According to a study by the National Community Reinvestment Coalition, the city has experienced more gentrification than any other U.S. city, with 40% of neighborhoods gentrified.[[150]](#cite_note-158)

As of 2010, about 17% of Washington, D.C. residents were age 18 or younger, which is lower than the U.S. average of 24%. However, at 34 years old, the district had the lowest median age compared to the 50 states as of 2010.[[151]](#cite_note-159) As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Washington,_D.C.&action=edit), there were an estimated 81,734 [immigrants](/wiki/Immigrants "Immigrants") living in Washington, D.C.[[152]](#cite_note-DCsocial-160) Major sources of immigration include [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala"), and [China](/wiki/China "China"), with a concentration of Salvadorans in the [Mount Pleasant](/wiki/Mount_Pleasant,_Washington,_D.C. "Mount Pleasant, Washington, D.C.") neighborhood.[[153]](#cite_note-161)

As of 2010, there were 4,822 same-sex couples in the city, about 2% of total households, according to [Williams Institute](/wiki/Williams_Institute "Williams Institute").[[154]](#cite_note-162) Legislation [authorizing same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_District_of_Columbia "Same-sex marriage in the District of Columbia") passed in 2009, and the district began issuing marriage licenses to same-sex couples in March 2010.[[155]](#cite_note-163)

Notable religious buildings in Washington, D.C.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Basilica_of_the_National_Shrine_of_the_Immaculate_Conception%2C_Washington.jpg/250px-Basilica_of_the_National_Shrine_of_the_Immaculate_Conception%2C_Washington.jpg)](/wiki/File:Basilica_of_the_National_Shrine_of_the_Immaculate_Conception,_Washington.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Islamic_Center%2C_Washington%2C_D.C_LCCN2011630761.jpg/250px-Islamic_Center%2C_Washington%2C_D.C_LCCN2011630761.jpg)](/wiki/File:Islamic_Center,_Washington,_D.C_LCCN2011630761.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/6th%26ISynagogue.jpg/250px-6th%26ISynagogue.jpg)](/wiki/File:6th%26ISynagogue.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_Lafayette_Square.jpg/250px-St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_Lafayette_Square.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church_Lafayette_Square.jpg)

Left to right from the top: The [Basilica of the National Shrine of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_National_Shrine_of_the_Immaculate_Conception "Basilica of the National Shrine of the Immaculate Conception"); [Islamic Center of Washington](/wiki/Islamic_Center_of_Washington "Islamic Center of Washington"); [Sixth & I Historic Synagogue](/wiki/Sixth_%26_I_Historic_Synagogue "Sixth & I Historic Synagogue"); and [St. John's Episcopal Church](/wiki/St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church,_Lafayette_Square "St. John's Episcopal Church, Lafayette Square")

As of 2007, about one-third of Washington, D.C., residents were [functionally illiterate](/wiki/Functionally_illiterate "Functionally illiterate"), greater than the national rate of about one-fifth. The city's relatively high illiteracy rate is attributed partly to immigrants who are not proficient in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language").[[156]](#cite_note-164) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Washington,_D.C.&action=edit), 85% of D.C. residents age 5 and older spoke English at home as a primary language.[[157]](#cite_note-165) Half of residents had at least a four-year college degree in 2006.[[152]](#cite_note-DCsocial-160) In 2017, the median household income in D.C. was $77,649;[[158]](#cite_note-DC_AF-166) also in 2017, D.C. residents had a [personal income per capita](/wiki/States_of_the_United_States_of_America_by_income "States of the United States of America by income") of $50,832 (higher than any of the 50 states).[[158]](#cite_note-DC_AF-166)[[159]](#cite_note-167) However, 19% of residents were below the poverty level in 2005, higher than any state except [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi"). In 2019, the poverty rate stood at 14.7%.[[160]](#cite_note-168)[[i]](#cite_note-170)[[162]](#cite_note-171)

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Washington,_D.C.&action=edit), more than 90% of Washington, D.C., residents had health insurance coverage, the second-highest rate in the nation. This is due in part to city programs that help provide insurance to low-income individuals who do not qualify for other types of coverage.[[163]](#cite_note-172)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] A 2009 report found that at least three percent of Washington, D.C., residents have [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV") or [AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS"), which the [Centers for Disease Control and Prevention](/wiki/Centers_for_Disease_Control_and_Prevention "Centers for Disease Control and Prevention") (CDC) characterizes as a "generalized and severe" epidemic.[[164]](#cite_note-173)

As of 2020, according to the [Association of Statisticians of American Religious Bodies](/wiki/Association_of_Statisticians_of_American_Religious_Bodies "Association of Statisticians of American Religious Bodies"), 56% of the city's residents were adherents[[j]](#cite_note-175) of a religious body. The largest tradition represented was [Evangelical Protestantism](/wiki/Evangelical_Protestantism "Evangelical Protestantism") (15% of total population), followed by [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") (12%), [Black Protestantism](/wiki/Black_Protestantism "Black Protestantism") (10%), [Mainline Protestantism](/wiki/Mainline_Protestantism "Mainline Protestantism") (10%), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") (3%), [Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") (2%), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (1%), and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (1%), with several other groups numbering less than 1%. Mainline Protestants were the largest group in 2010, Catholics in 2000, and Black Protestants in 1990.[[165]](#cite_note-ARDA-174) The city is populated with many religious buildings, including [Washington National Cathedral](/wiki/Washington_National_Cathedral "Washington National Cathedral"), [Basilica of the National Shrine of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_National_Shrine_of_the_Immaculate_Conception "Basilica of the National Shrine of the Immaculate Conception"), which comprises the [largest Catholic church building in the United States](/wiki/List_of_largest_church_buildings "List of largest church buildings"), the [Islamic Center of Washington](/wiki/Islamic_Center_of_Washington "Islamic Center of Washington"), which was the largest [mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") in the Western Hemisphere when it opened in 1957, and [DSK Mariam Church](/wiki/DSK_Mariam_Church "DSK Mariam Church"), one of the oldest Ethiopian Orthodox congregations in the United States. [St. John's Episcopal Church](/wiki/St._John%27s_Episcopal_Church,_Lafayette_Square "St. John's Episcopal Church, Lafayette Square"), located off [Lafayette Square](/wiki/Lafayette_Square_(Washington,_D.C.) "Lafayette Square (Washington, D.C.)"), has held services for every [U.S. president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") since [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"). The [Sixth & I Historic Synagogue](/wiki/Sixth_%26_I_Historic_Synagogue "Sixth & I Historic Synagogue"), built in 1908, is a [synagogue](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") located in the [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Washington,_D.C. "Chinatown, Washington, D.C.") section of the city. The [Washington D.C. Temple](/wiki/Washington_D.C._Temple "Washington D.C. Temple") is a [temple](/wiki/Temple_(LDS_Church) "Temple (LDS Church)") of [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints"), located just outside the city in [Kensington, Maryland](/wiki/Kensington,_Maryland "Kensington, Maryland"). Viewable from the [Capital Beltway](/wiki/Capital_Beltway "Capital Beltway"), the temple is the tallest temple of the church in existence, and is the third-largest by square footage.[[166]](#cite_note-176)[[167]](#cite_note-177)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of the Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Washington_metropolitan_area "Economy of the Washington metropolitan area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Ec_05_%2826114196145%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Ec_05_%2826114196145%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ec_05_(26114196145)_(cropped).jpg)

The [Eccles Building](/wiki/Eccles_Building "Eccles Building") on [Constitution Avenue](/wiki/Constitution_Avenue "Constitution Avenue"), home of the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve"), the [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") of the United States

As of 2011,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Washington,_D.C.&action=edit) the [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"), including the District of Columbia as well as parts of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), and [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia"), was the nation's eighth-largest metropolitan economy. Its growing and diversified economy has an increasing percentage of professional and business service jobs in addition to more traditional jobs rooted in tourism, entertainment, and government.[[168]](#cite_note-178)[*[obsolete source](/wiki/Wikipedia:AGE_MATTERS "Wikipedia:AGE MATTERS")*]

Between 2009 and 2016, [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") per capita in Washington, D.C., consistently ranked at the very top among U.S. states.[[169]](#cite_note-capita2009-179) In 2016, at $160,472, its GDP per capita was almost three times greater than that of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), which was ranked second in the nation (see [List of U.S. states and territories by GDP](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_GDP "List of U.S. states and territories by GDP")).[[169]](#cite_note-capita2009-179)
As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Washington,_D.C.&action=edit), the metropolitan statistical area's unemployment rate was 3.1%, ranking 171 out of the 389 metropolitan areas as defined by the U.S. [Bureau of Economic Analysis](/wiki/Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis "Bureau of Economic Analysis").[[170]](#cite_note-180) The District of Columbia itself had an unemployment rate of 4.6% during the same time period.[[171]](#cite_note-181) In 2019, Washington, D.C., had the highest median household income in the U.S. at $92,266.[[172]](#cite_note-182)

According to the District's [comprehensive annual financial reports](/wiki/Comprehensive_annual_financial_report "Comprehensive annual financial report"), the top employers by number of employees in 2022 included [Georgetown University](/wiki/Georgetown_University "Georgetown University"), [Children's National Medical Center](/wiki/Children%27s_National_Medical_Center "Children's National Medical Center"), [Washington Hospital Center](/wiki/Washington_Hospital_Center "Washington Hospital Center"), [George Washington University](/wiki/George_Washington_University "George Washington University"), [American University](/wiki/American_University "American University"), [Georgetown University Hospital](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Hospital "Georgetown University Hospital"), [Booz Allen & Hamilton](/wiki/Booz_Allen_%26_Hamilton "Booz Allen & Hamilton"), [Insperity](/wiki/Insperity "Insperity") PEO Services, [Universal Protection Service](/wiki/Universal_Protection_Service "Universal Protection Service"), [Howard University](/wiki/Howard_University "Howard University"), [Medstar Medical Group](/wiki/MedStar_Health "MedStar Health"), [George Washington University Hospital](/wiki/George_Washington_University_Hospital "George Washington University Hospital"), [Catholic University of America](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_America "Catholic University of America"), and [Sibley Memorial Hospital](/wiki/Sibley_Memorial_Hospital "Sibley Memorial Hospital").[[173]](#cite_note-183)

### Federal government

Main article: [Federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Aerial_view_showing_top_of_Washington_Monument%2C_Washington%2C_D.C_LCCN2010630904_%28cropped%29.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Aerial_view_showing_top_of_Washington_Monument%2C_Washington%2C_D.C_LCCN2010630904_%28cropped%29.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_showing_top_of_Washington_Monument,_Washington,_D.C_LCCN2010630904_(cropped).tiff)

[Federal Triangle](/wiki/Federal_Triangle "Federal Triangle"), a historic hub of [executive departments](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") of the [U.S. federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States")

As of July 2022, 25% of people employed in Washington, D.C., were employed by the federal government.[[174]](#cite_note-184) Many of the region's residents are employed by companies and organizations that do work for the federal government, seek to [influence federal policy](/wiki/Lobbying_in_the_United_States "Lobbying in the United States"), or are otherwise related to its work, including [law firms](/wiki/Law_firm "Law firm"), [defense contractors](/wiki/Defense_contractor "Defense contractor"), [civilian contractors](/wiki/Independent_contractor "Independent contractor"), [nonprofit organizations](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization"), [lobbying firms](/wiki/Lobbying_in_the_United_States "Lobbying in the United States"), [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union"), [industry trade groups](/wiki/Industry_trade_group "Industry trade group"), and [professional associations](/wiki/Professional_association "Professional association"), many of which have their headquarters in or near the city for proximity to the federal government.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Research and non-profit organizations

See also: [Category:Non-profit organizations based in Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Category:Non-profit_organizations_based_in_Washington,_D.C. "Category:Non-profit organizations based in Washington, D.C.")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/American_Enterprise_Institute_%28AEI%29_%2853821618777%29.jpg/250px-American_Enterprise_Institute_%28AEI%29_%2853821618777%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:American_Enterprise_Institute_(AEI)_(53821618777).jpg)

The [American Enterprise Institute](/wiki/American_Enterprise_Institute "American Enterprise Institute"), one of the city's many [think tanks](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank")

Washington, D.C., is a leading center for national and international research organizations, especially [think tanks](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank") engaged in public policy.[[175]](#cite_note-185) As of 2020, 8% of the country's think tanks are based in the city, including many of the largest and most widely cited,[[176]](#cite_note-186) including the [Carnegie Endowment for International Peace](/wiki/Carnegie_Endowment_for_International_Peace "Carnegie Endowment for International Peace"), [Center for Strategic and International Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Strategic_and_International_Studies "Center for Strategic and International Studies"), [Peterson Institute for International Economics](/wiki/Peterson_Institute_for_International_Economics "Peterson Institute for International Economics"), [The Heritage Foundation](/wiki/The_Heritage_Foundation "The Heritage Foundation"), and [Urban Institute](/wiki/Urban_Institute "Urban Institute").[[177]](#cite_note-187)

Washington, D.C. is home to many non-profit organizations that engage with issues of domestic and global importance by conducting advanced research, running programs, or public advocacy. Among these organizations are the [UN Foundation](/wiki/UN_Foundation "UN Foundation"), [Human Rights Campaign](/wiki/Human_Rights_Campaign "Human Rights Campaign"), [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"), and the [National Endowment for Democracy](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_Democracy "National Endowment for Democracy").[[178]](#cite_note-188) Major medical research institutions include the [MedStar Washington Hospital Center](/wiki/MedStar_Washington_Hospital_Center "MedStar Washington Hospital Center") and the [Children's National Medical Center](/wiki/Children%27s_National_Medical_Center "Children's National Medical Center").[[179]](#cite_note-189)

The city is the country's primary location for international development firms, many of which contract with the D.C.-based [United States Agency for International Development](/wiki/United_States_Agency_for_International_Development "United States Agency for International Development") (USAID), the U.S. federal government's aid agency. The [American Red Cross](/wiki/American_Red_Cross "American Red Cross"), a humanitarian agency focused on emergency relief, is also based in the city.[[180]](#cite_note-190)

### Private sector

See also: [Category:Companies based in Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Category:Companies_based_in_Washington,_D.C. "Category:Companies based in Washington, D.C.") and [Washington metropolitan area § Primary industries](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area#Primary_industries "Washington metropolitan area")

According to statistics compiled in 2011, four of the [largest 500 companies](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") in the country were based in Washington, D.C.[[181]](#cite_note-191) In the 2023 [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index"), Washington was ranked as having the 8th most competitive financial center in the world, and fourth most competitive in the United States (after [New York City](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City"), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco#Economy "San Francisco"), and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles#Economy "Los Angeles")).[[182]](#cite_note-192) Among the largest companies based in Washington, D.C., are [Fannie Mae](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae"), [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), [Danaher Corporation](/wiki/Danaher_Corporation "Danaher Corporation"), [FTI Consulting](/wiki/FTI_Consulting "FTI Consulting"), and [Hogan Lovells](/wiki/Hogan_Lovells "Hogan Lovells").[[183]](#cite_note-193)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Tourism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/World_War_II_Memorial_Wade-47.JPG/250px-World_War_II_Memorial_Wade-47.JPG)](/wiki/File:World_War_II_Memorial_Wade-47.JPG)

The [World War II Memorial](/wiki/World_War_II_Memorial "World War II Memorial"), one of many popular tourist sites located on the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall")

Tourism is the city's second-largest industry, after the federal government. In 2012, some 18.9 million visitors contributed an estimated $4.8 billion to the local economy.[[184]](#cite_note-tourism2012-194) In 2019, the city saw 24.6 million tourists, including 1.8 million from foreign countries, who collectively spent $8.15 billion during their stay.[[185]](#cite_note-tourism2019-195) Tourism helps many of the region's other industries, such as lodging, food and beverage, entertainment, shopping, and transportation.[[185]](#cite_note-tourism2019-195)

The city and the larger [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area") have an array of attractions for tourists, including monuments, memorials, museums, sports events, and trails. Within the city, the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall") serves as the center of the tourism industry. It is there that many of the city's museums and monuments are located. Adjacent to the mall sits the [Tidal Basin](/wiki/Tidal_Basin "Tidal Basin"), where several major national memorials and monuments are located, including the popular [Jefferson Memorial](/wiki/Jefferson_Memorial "Jefferson Memorial"). [Washington Union Station](/wiki/Washington_Union_Station "Washington Union Station") is a popular tourist spot with its multitude of restaurants and shops.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
